nodes.rationale: Attributing logistics costs to the activities and cost drivers that generate them, enabling accurate customer and product profitability analysis.

nodes.label: Market-Driven Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Designing the supply chain from the customer backwards by identifying value segments, defining the value proposition and aligning supply chain strategy.

nodes.label: Customer Service Prioritization
nodes.rationale: Treating service as a scarce resource and allocating the highest service levels to the most profitable key customers and products.

nodes.label: ABC Classification
nodes.rationale: Categorizing products/customers into A, B and C tiers by profit contribution to differentiate inventory and service policies.

nodes.label: Cost to Serve
nodes.rationale: The full cost incurred in servicing a particular customer, which can vary widely even between customers making equivalent purchases.

nodes.label: Profit Contribution Analysis
nodes.rationale: Measuring the difference between revenue and directly attributable costs at SKU level rather than relying on sales or volume.

nodes.label: Differentiated Service Levels
nodes.rationale: Setting different stock availability and positioning policies per product/customer category to match profitability and demand.

nodes.label: Centralised Inventory
nodes.rationale: Holding slow-moving but profitable items at a central location far up the supply chain and shipping by express transport to cut total inventory.

nodes.label: Critical Value Analysis
nodes.rationale: Weighting service priority by how critical an item is to the customer (e.g. spare parts where downtime costs are high), adjusting profit-based rankings.

nodes.label: Customer Service Standards
nodes.rationale: Predetermined, customer-defined performance standards (order cycle time, availability, reliability, etc.) against which service is measured.

nodes.label: Order Cycle Time
nodes.rationale: The elapsed time from customer order placement to delivery, a core customer service standard.

nodes.label: Stock Availability
nodes.rationale: Percentage of demand for a line item (SKU) that can be met from available inventory.

nodes.label: Order Fill Rate
nodes.rationale: Proportion of complete orders satisfied; multiplicative probability across line items makes perfect order fill hard to achieve.

nodes.label: Composite Service Index
nodes.rationale: A weighted index combining multiple service measures by their customer-perceived importance to communicate overall performance internally.

nodes.label: Pre/Transaction/Post-Transaction Framework
nodes.rationale: Framework grouping customer service measures into pre-transaction, transaction and post-transaction elements for monitoring.

nodes.label: Return on Investment (ROI)
nodes.source_location: Ch.3 Measuring Logistics Costs and Performance
nodes.rationale: Ratio of net profit to capital employed, decomposable into margin and asset turn; logistics can improve both levers.

nodes.label: Asset Turn (Capital Turnover)
nodes.source_location: Ch.3 Measuring Logistics Costs and Performance
nodes.rationale: Sales divided by capital employed; improving capital productivity can boost ROI more effectively than chasing margin alone.

nodes.label: Logistics Impact on the Balance Sheet
nodes.source_location: Ch.3 Measuring Logistics Costs and Performance
nodes.rationale: How logistics variables (order cycle, invoice accuracy, inventory, fixed assets) shape cash, receivables, inventory and capital structure.
nodes.norm_label: logistics impact on the balance sheet

nodes.label: Economic Value Added (EVA)
nodes.source_location: Ch.3 Measuring Logistics Costs and Performance
nodes.rationale: Profit after tax less the true cost of capital employed; sustained negative EVA erodes shareholder value.

nodes.label: Market Value Added (MVA)
nodes.source_location: Ch.3 Measuring Logistics Costs and Performance
nodes.rationale: Net present value of expected future EVA; a measure of the wealth a business represents to its shareholders.

nodes.label: Drivers of Shareholder Value
nodes.source_location: Ch.3 Measuring Logistics Costs and Performance
nodes.rationale: Five levers - revenue growth, operating cost reduction, fixed capital efficiency, working capital efficiency, tax minimisation - all influenced by logistics.

nodes.label: Free Cash Flow
nodes.source_location: Ch.3 Measuring Logistics Costs and Performance
nodes.rationale: After-tax operating cash less working and fixed capital investment; strategies are judged by how they enhance or accelerate it.

nodes.label: Cash-to-Cash Cycle Time
nodes.source_location: Ch.3 Measuring Logistics Costs and Performance
nodes.rationale: Elapsed time from procurement of materials to sale of finished product; reducing it through time compression frees working capital.

nodes.label: Working Capital Efficiency
nodes.source_location: Ch.3 Measuring Logistics Costs and Performance
nodes.rationale: Minimising inventory, receivables and pipeline length to reduce capital tied up while sustaining service.

nodes.label: Logistics Cost Trade-offs
nodes.source_location: Ch.3 Measuring Logistics Costs and Performance
nodes.rationale: Balancing increases in one cost area against savings or revenue gains in another so total cost effectiveness improves.

nodes.label: Logistics Cost Analysis
nodes.source_location: Ch.3 Measuring Logistics Costs and Performance
nodes.rationale: Flow-oriented costing of the pipeline from suppliers to customers, since conventional accounting distorts true product and customer profitability.

nodes.label: Cost of Holding Inventory
nodes.source_location: Ch.3 Measuring Logistics Costs and Performance
nodes.rationale: True annual cost of carrying stock (~25% of book value) including cost of capital, storage, obsolescence, deterioration, pilferage and insurance.

nodes.label: Mission Costing
nodes.source_location: Ch.3 Measuring Logistics Costs and Performance
nodes.rationale: Output-oriented costing that determines functional budgets from the customer-service missions they serve, cutting across functional lines.

nodes.label: Attributable Cost
nodes.source_location: Ch.3 Measuring Logistics Costs and Performance
nodes.rationale: Cost per unit avoidable if a product or function were discontinued without changing the supporting organisation structure.

nodes.label: Customer Profitability Matrix
nodes.source_location: Ch.3 Measuring Logistics Costs and Performance
nodes.rationale: Classifying customers by net sales value vs cost-to-serve into Build, Danger Zone, Cost Engineer and Protect strategies.

nodes.label: Direct Product Profitability (DPP)
nodes.source_location: Ch.3 Measuring Logistics Costs and Performance
nodes.rationale: Retail-popular technique attaching all distribution and handling costs to a product/order to reveal net profit beyond gross margin.

nodes.label: Cost Drivers
nodes.source_location: Ch.3 Measuring Logistics Costs and Performance
nodes.rationale: The activities along the logistics pipeline (e.g. order lines, deliveries) that cause costs because they consume resources.

nodes.label: Matching Supply and Demand
nodes.rationale: The fundamental goal of supply chain management, made difficult by uncertainty on both supply and demand sides.

nodes.label: Lead-Time Gap
nodes.rationale: Difference between the logistics lead time to make/deliver and the shorter time the customer is prepared to wait, traditionally bridged by inventory.

nodes.label: Visibility of Demand
nodes.rationale: Seeing real final-marketplace demand rather than distorted derived demand or orders; a foundation of responsiveness.

nodes.label: Demand Penetration Point (Decoupling Point)
nodes.rationale: The point where real demand meets the plan; upstream is forecast-driven, downstream is demand-driven, and strategic inventory is held there.

nodes.label: Strategic (Generic) Inventory
nodes.rationale: Inventory held in generic, unfinished form at the decoupling point to enable late configuration against actual demand.

nodes.label: Information Iceberg
nodes.rationale: Metaphor that an order is only the visible tip of demand while ongoing consumption/usage stays hidden from the supplier.

nodes.label: Feed-Forward of Demand Information
nodes.rationale: Sharing ongoing usage/consumption data so suppliers anticipate requirements and schedule logistics ahead of orders.

nodes.label: Supply Chain Fulcrum
nodes.rationale: The commitment point where volume/mix decisions are made; moving it closer to demand reduces required inventory and capacity.

nodes.label: Supply Chain Velocity
nodes.rationale: Speed of supply chain response - through streamlined processes and reduced non-value-adding time - which with visibility drives responsiveness.

nodes.label: Responsive Supply Chain
nodes.source_location: Ch.5 Creating the Responsive Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: A supply chain built on visibility and velocity that can react rapidly to real demand in volatile markets.

nodes.label: Forecast for Capacity, Execute Against Demand
nodes.rationale: Forecasting at aggregate volume to plan capacity/resources while configuring or making final product against actual customer orders.
nodes.norm_label: forecast for capacity, execute against demand

nodes.label: Demand Management
nodes.rationale: Tools and procedures that balance supply and demand through deeper understanding of the causes of demand volatility, including proactive shaping.

nodes.label: Sales and Operations Planning (S&OP)
nodes.rationale: A formalised cross-functional process aligning demand creation and fulfilment via consensus forecasts and rough-cut capacity planning.

nodes.label: Consensus Forecast
nodes.rationale: A single agreed forecast produced by sales/marketing and operations jointly within the S&OP process.

nodes.label: Aggregate-Level Forecasting
nodes.rationale: Forecasting at product-family/aggregate volume level for capacity planning, since it is more accurate than item-level long-horizon forecasts.

nodes.label: Collaborative Planning, Forecasting and Replenishment (CPFR)
nodes.rationale: Partnership approach where buyer and supplier share information and jointly create agreed forecasts and replenishment decisions.

nodes.label: Vendor Managed Inventory (VMI)
nodes.rationale: Process where the supplier manages flow into the customer's operations using off-take data, substituting information for inventory.

nodes.label: Agile Supply Chain
nodes.source_location: Ch.5 Creating the Responsive Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: A supply chain that is market-sensitive, virtual, process-aligned and network-based to deliver responsiveness to real demand.

nodes.label: Lean Thinking
nodes.source_location: Ch.5 Creating the Responsive Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Doing more with less by eliminating waste with level schedules; works best in high-volume, low-variety, predictable environments.

nodes.label: Leagile (Lean/Agile Hybrid)
nodes.source_location: Ch.5 Creating the Responsive Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Building agile response on a lean platform - lean up to the decoupling point and agile after it - when lead times cannot be reduced.

nodes.label: Generic Supply Chain Strategies
nodes.source_location: Ch.5 Creating the Responsive Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Four strategies (Kanban, lean, agile/quick response, hybrid) selected by combining supply lead time and demand predictability.

nodes.label: Quick Response
nodes.source_location: Ch.5 Creating the Responsive Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Agile solution for unpredictable demand with short lead times, extreme case being rapid make-to-order.

nodes.label: Make-to-Order
nodes.source_location: Ch.5 Creating the Responsive Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Producing only against known customer orders, the ideal toward which delaying the commitment point moves the supply chain.

nodes.label: Build-to-Order
nodes.rationale: Strategy (exemplified by Dell) of building customised products to order using modular design and tight supplier synchronisation.

nodes.label: Market Sensitivity
nodes.source_location: Ch.5 Creating the Responsive Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Capability of a supply chain to read and respond to real demand rather than relying on forecasts.

nodes.label: Virtual Supply Chain
nodes.source_location: Ch.5 Creating the Responsive Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: An information-based rather than inventory-based supply chain where partners act on shared real-demand data via EDI and the Internet.

nodes.label: Process Alignment
nodes.source_location: Ch.5 Creating the Responsive Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Collaborative working - joint development, common systems, shared information, open-book accounting - between buyers and suppliers.

nodes.label: Network-Based Competition
nodes.source_location: Ch.5 Creating the Responsive Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Recognition that businesses compete as supply chains/networks of partners rather than stand-alone entities.

nodes.label: Push versus Pull
nodes.source_location: Ch.5 Creating the Responsive Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Contrast between push systems building to forecast as buffers and pull systems where end demand pulls product and components through.

nodes.label: Kanban
nodes.source_location: Ch.5 Creating the Responsive Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Japanese pull-based continuous replenishment system that signals upstream release and progressively reduces inventory to expose bottlenecks.

nodes.label: Toyota Production System (TPS)
nodes.source_location: Ch.5 Creating the Responsive Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Origin of lean thinking, preoccupied with reduction or elimination of waste (muda) through level schedules.

nodes.label: Waste (Muda)
nodes.source_location: Ch.5 Creating the Responsive Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: In the Japanese philosophy inventory is waste that merely hides problems; lowering it forces management to confront root causes.

nodes.label: Dependent vs Independent Demand
nodes.source_location: Ch.5 Creating the Responsive Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Independent demand can be forecast traditionally while dependent demand must be calculated from demand at the next level in the chain.

nodes.label: Time Compression
nodes.source_location: Ch.3 Measuring Logistics Costs and Performance
nodes.rationale: Reducing non-value-adding time in the supply chain to enhance service and cut cost and working capital simultaneously.

nodes.label: Third-Party Logistics (3PL)
nodes.source_location: Ch.3 Measuring Logistics Costs and Performance
nodes.rationale: Outsourcing warehousing and transport to move fixed assets off the balance sheet and improve fixed capital efficiency.

nodes.label: Supply Chain Synchronisation
nodes.source_location: Ch.5 Creating the Responsive Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: All parties in the chain 'march to the same drumbeat' via shared information and process alignment, acting as one synchronized network.

nodes.label: Shared Information
nodes.rationale: Sharing of demand, inventory, capacity and schedule data across supply chain partners to enable synchronization and visibility.

nodes.label: Point-of-Sale Data
nodes.rationale: Real demand data captured at retail checkout (e.g. via EPOS) and shared upstream to drive replenishment and production.

nodes.label: Business Process Re-engineering (BPR)
nodes.source_location: Ch.5 Creating the Responsive Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Simplifying and reshaping organisational processes to achieve outcomes in shorter time-frames at less cost, often by running activities in parallel.

nodes.label: Non-Value-Adding Time
nodes.rationale: Time spent on activities that create cost but no customer benefit; idle time spent as inventory is the classic example.

nodes.label: Value-Adding Time
nodes.rationale: Time spent creating time, place and form utility for which the customer is prepared to pay.

nodes.label: Spatial Postponement
nodes.source_location: Ch.5 Creating the Responsive Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Holding inventory in few locations with ability to ship rapidly to where demand arises, rather than pre-positioning final form.

nodes.label: Process Management
nodes.source_location: Ch.5 Creating the Responsive Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Managing horizontal, market-facing, cross-functional sequences of value-creating activities rather than vertical functional silos.

nodes.label: Functional Silos
nodes.rationale: Inwardly focused departmental structures with multiple hand-offs that slow response to market change and inhibit agility.

nodes.label: Cross-Functional Teams
nodes.source_location: Ch.5 Creating the Responsive Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Interdisciplinary teams that manage market-facing processes horizontally to improve speed and reliability of response.

nodes.label: Performance Metrics
nodes.source_location: Ch.5 Creating the Responsive Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Measurement systems shape behaviour; time-based and customer-based metrics encourage agile practices over cost/utilisation efficiency.

nodes.label: Customer Responsiveness
nodes.source_location: Ch.5 Creating the Responsive Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: A fundamental tenet of agility: the ability to respond rapidly to changing customer needs and become the supplier of choice.

nodes.label: Shortening Life Cycles
nodes.rationale: Compression of product life spans that reduces time to develop, launch and meet demand, raising obsolescence risk.

nodes.label: Order-to-Delivery Cycle
nodes.rationale: Order cycle time (OCT): elapsed time from receipt of a customer order to delivery; reliability can matter more than length.

nodes.label: Cash-to-Cash Cycle
nodes.rationale: Total time working capital is committed from procurement of materials to receipt of customer payment.

nodes.label: Logistics Pipeline Management
nodes.rationale: Linking manufacturing and procurement lead times to marketplace needs to lower cost, raise quality and flexibility, and speed response.

nodes.label: Supply Chain Mapping
nodes.rationale: Time-based representation distinguishing horizontal (process) time from vertical (inventory) time to expose non-value-adding time.

nodes.label: Throughput Efficiency
nodes.rationale: Value-added time divided by end-to-end pipeline time times 100; often as low as 10 percent in real supply chains.

nodes.label: Bottleneck Management
nodes.rationale: Focusing on the slowest activity that determines total throughput time; non-bottlenecks must be governed by bottleneck requirements.

nodes.label: Drum-Buffer-Rope
nodes.rationale: TOC scheduling: drum sets pace, buffer protects the bottleneck, rope signals replenishment to the front of the chain.

nodes.label: Set-Up Time Reduction (SMED)
nodes.rationale: Single Minute Exchange of Die; reducing changeover time toward zero to enable flexible response without inventory penalty.

nodes.label: Synchronous Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: A network where each stage is connected via shared information so the chain acts as one rather than disconnected islands.

nodes.label: Collaborative Working
nodes.rationale: Co-operative partner relationships, more effective than adversarial arm's-length dealings, enabling visibility and process integration.

nodes.label: In-Bound Consolidation
nodes.rationale: Combining orders from multiple suppliers into single deliveries via a hub to enable frequent small shipments economically.

nodes.label: Cross-Docking
nodes.rationale: Stockless transhipment where store orders are sorted at a cross-dock for immediate onward delivery, achieving JIT with low stock.

nodes.label: Continuous Replenishment
nodes.rationale: Products rapidly replenished after they are sold based on frequent demand signals, replacing forecast-driven push systems.

nodes.label: Flexible Manufacturing Systems (FMS)
nodes.rationale: Manufacturing flexibility enabling response to varied demand; the main barrier is changeover (set-up) time, not technology alone.

nodes.label: Mass Customisation
nodes.rationale: Delivering tailored solutions for specific customer requirements without raising finished-goods inventory, often via postponement.

nodes.label: Industrial Dynamics
nodes.rationale: Forrester's study of information-feedback characteristics where structure, amplification and time delays interact to affect performance.

nodes.label: Acceleration Effect
nodes.rationale: Self-generated fluctuations where small consumer demand changes cause magnified swings in factory orders via reorder policies.

nodes.label: Promotional Activity Distortion
nodes.rationale: Trade promotions and forward buying trigger Forrester-type surges and hidden logistics costs, with few promotions actually profitable.

nodes.label: Logistics Information System
nodes.rationale: Integrated planning, control, coordination and customer-service information that drives competitive logistics strategy.

nodes.label: Electronic Data Interchange / Extranets
nodes.rationale: Internet/extranet-based data exchange (EPOS, bar coding) letting partners share information cheaply to enable QR and synchronization.

nodes.label: Network Complexity
nodes.source_location: Ch.8 Complexity and the Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: More nodes and links, multiplied by reliance on multi-tier suppliers, raising the potential for unexpected disruption.

nodes.label: Range Complexity
nodes.source_location: Ch.8 Complexity and the Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Proliferation of products, variants and pack sizes that lowers average demand per variant and worsens forecast accuracy.

nodes.label: Product Complexity
nodes.source_location: Ch.8 Complexity and the Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Design-driven complexity from many components or low Bill-of-Materials commonality, constraining mix/volume flexibility.

nodes.label: Component Commonality
nodes.source_location: Ch.8 Complexity and the Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Sharing components, sub-assemblies and platforms across products to reduce complexity and increase flexibility.

nodes.label: Customer Complexity
nodes.source_location: Ch.8 Complexity and the Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Complexity from too many non-standard service options and customised solutions, with widely varying cost-to-serve.

nodes.label: Supplier Complexity
nodes.source_location: Ch.8 Complexity and the Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Large supplier base raising relationships and transaction costs; rationalisation aids collaboration but can raise risk.

nodes.label: Supplier Development
nodes.source_location: Ch.8 Complexity and the Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Working closely with a smaller supplier base to jointly improve product quality, process quality and reduce cost.

nodes.label: Long Tail
nodes.source_location: Ch.8 Complexity and the Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Large number of slow-moving SKUs/customers contributing little profit; candidates for rationalisation or virtual-inventory strategies.

nodes.label: Range Rationalisation
nodes.source_location: Ch.8 Complexity and the Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Selective elimination of slow-moving lines to improve profitability, balanced cautiously against strategic variety needs.

nodes.label: Product Design for Supply Chain
nodes.source_location: Ch.8 Complexity and the Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: The supply chain 'begins on the drawing board'; up to 80 percent of through-life costs are set by design decisions.

nodes.label: Offshoring / Low-Cost Country Sourcing
nodes.source_location: Ch.8 Complexity and the Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Sourcing from distant low-cost sources that lengthens replenishment lead times and adds organisational and risk complexity.

nodes.label: Supply Chain Vulnerability
nodes.source_location: Ch.8 Complexity and the Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Exposure to disruption from interconnected global networks, illustrated by the volcanic ash cloud and earthquake supply shocks.

nodes.label: Managing the Global Pipeline
nodes.rationale: Coordinating and controlling worldwide flows of materials and inventory across geographically dispersed sourcing, production and distribution to balance global cost economies against local service needs.

nodes.label: Global Business
nodes.rationale: A business that does more than export: it sources components in multiple countries, has geographically dispersed assembly, and markets products worldwide (e.g. Nike, Nestle).

nodes.label: Offshore / Global Sourcing
nodes.rationale: Procuring materials or products from low-cost overseas locations; nominal price savings can be offset by transport, inventory, lead-time, obsolescence and quality costs (total cost of ownership).

nodes.label: Focused Factory
nodes.rationale: Limiting the range and mix of products made at a single site to achieve economies of scale, serving regional or global demand from fewer plants rather than local-for-local production.

nodes.label: Square Root Rule (Inventory)
nodes.rationale: Rule of thumb that total safety stock reduction from consolidating locations is proportional to the square root of the ratio of the number of stock locations before and after rationalisation.

nodes.label: Virtual / Electronic Inventory
nodes.rationale: Locating inventory physically near customers or production but managing and controlling it centrally via information, achieving centralisation's stock reduction while keeping local flexibility.

nodes.label: Design for Localisation
nodes.rationale: Engineering products and processes so semi-finished product can be configured and finished locally, maximising variety from the smallest number of standard modules.

nodes.label: Thinking Global, Acting Local
nodes.rationale: Centralising strategic structuring, network design and information control for worldwide cost optimisation while localising customer service management against specific market needs.

nodes.label: Supply Chain Event Management (SCEM)
nodes.rationale: Monitoring the planned sequence of supply chain activities and reporting/responding to deviations, using intelligent agents and control limits like an intensive-care monitor to flag exceptions.

nodes.label: End-to-End Lead-Time Variability
nodes.rationale: Uncertainty in global pipelines from shipping, consolidation and customs clearance, which inflates buffer stocks as local managers over-order to compensate for unreliable transit times.

nodes.label: Substitute Information for Inventory
nodes.rationale: Using timely demand information flowing from point of sale into replenishment systems to reduce dependency on inventory; time lapses in information translate directly into inventory.

nodes.label: Key Account Management (KAM)
nodes.rationale: Tailoring a supplier's full resources to deliver customer-specific solutions to global customers, replacing a one-size-fits-all approach to customer service.

nodes.label: Outsource Execution, Not Control
nodes.rationale: Lesson from Boeing 787: a company may outsource the execution of an activity but should never outsource its control of that activity.

nodes.label: Supply Chain Risk
nodes.source_location: Chapter 10: Managing risk in the supply chain
nodes.rationale: Exposure to disruption expressed as probability of disruption multiplied by impact; the biggest continuity risks often lie in the wider supply network rather than internal IT or finance.

nodes.label: Supply Chain Risk Profile
nodes.source_location: Chapter 10: Managing risk in the supply chain
nodes.rationale: A business-specific map of where the greatest vulnerabilities lie and the probability of disruption, used to focus attention on critical paths across the network.

nodes.label: Five Sources of Supply Chain Risk
nodes.source_location: Chapter 10: Managing risk in the supply chain
nodes.rationale: Audit framework of supply, demand, process, control and environmental risk used to assess where a business is vulnerable to disruption.
nodes.norm_label: five sources of supply chain risk

nodes.label: Demand Risk
nodes.source_location: Chapter 10: Managing risk in the supply chain
nodes.rationale: Risk arising from demand volatility, bullwhip-driven demand amplification and parallel interactions where demand for one product affects another.

nodes.label: Control Risk
nodes.source_location: Chapter 10: Managing risk in the supply chain
nodes.rationale: Risk from a firm's own internal control systems - order quantities, batch sizes and safety stock rules that distort real demand and create chaos-type effects.

nodes.label: Risk Mitigation (Redundancy vs Flexibility)
nodes.source_location: Chapter 10: Managing risk in the supply chain
nodes.rationale: Plans to reduce risk fall into redundancy (extra inventory, backups, long-term contracts) and flexibility (agile design, transferable manufacturing, lead-time reduction, visibility, cross-training).

nodes.label: Critical Paths (Nodes and Links)
nodes.source_location: Chapter 10: Managing risk in the supply chain
nodes.rationale: Identifying mission-critical nodes and links in a network - long lead times, single sources, bottlenecks, concentration - whose failure would most severely affect performance.

nodes.label: Supply Chain Risk Management Process
nodes.source_location: Chapter 10: Managing risk in the supply chain
nodes.rationale: Seven-stage approach: understand the chain, improve it, identify critical paths, manage critical paths, improve visibility, establish a continuity team, and work with suppliers/customers.

nodes.label: Failure Mode and Effect Analysis (FMEA)
nodes.source_location: Chapter 10: Managing risk in the supply chain
nodes.rationale: Systematic tool scoring severity, likelihood of occurrence and likelihood of detection for each node/link, multiplying scores to prioritise where to reduce failure risk.

nodes.label: Process Variability Reduction
nodes.source_location: Chapter 10: Managing risk in the supply chain
nodes.rationale: Reducing variation in supply chain processes through process control so the consistency and reliability of output can be near-guaranteed, lowering risk.

nodes.label: Cause and Effect / Root Cause Analysis
nodes.source_location: Chapter 10: Managing risk in the supply chain
nodes.rationale: Separating symptoms from underlying causes through progressive questioning ('asking why five times') to remove or avoid the real causes of supply chain problems.
nodes.norm_label: cause and effect / root cause analysis

nodes.label: Supply Chain Continuity Team
nodes.source_location: Chapter 10: Managing risk in the supply chain
nodes.rationale: A cross-functional team reporting to board level that maintains a risk register and carries out the analysis and implementation of supply chain risk management.

nodes.label: Single Sourcing / Supplier Base Reduction Risk
nodes.source_location: Chapter 10: Managing risk in the supply chain
nodes.rationale: Reducing the supplier base or relying on a sole source brings benefits but increases vulnerability, as a failure at a single source can disrupt the whole chain.
nodes.norm_label: single sourcing / supplier base reduction risk

nodes.label: Radio Frequency Identification (RFID)
nodes.source_location: Chapter 10: Managing risk in the supply chain
nodes.rationale: Active or passive tags enabling supply chain track-and-trace capability, improving visibility as costs fall and major buyers like Wal-Mart mandate adoption.

nodes.label: Business Continuity Management
nodes.source_location: Chapter 10: Managing risk in the supply chain
nodes.rationale: Organisational practice of preparing for disruption; Christopher argues it must widen beyond IT and internal process focus to the broader supply network.

nodes.label: Network / Virtual Organisation
nodes.source_location: Chapter 11: The era of network competition
nodes.rationale: A confederation of specialist firms linked through shared information and aligned processes that satisfies customer needs more effectively than a single vertically integrated firm.

nodes.label: Supply Chain Collaboration
nodes.source_location: Chapter 11: The era of network competition
nodes.rationale: High levels of cooperation, joint strategy and information sharing across the network; illustrated by the prisoner's dilemma showing trust-based cooperation beats adversarial behaviour.

nodes.label: Co-makership / Partnership Sourcing
nodes.source_location: Chapter 11: The era of network competition
nodes.rationale: Developing long-term relationships with a limited number of suppliers based on mutual confidence, treating the supplier as an extension of the customer's operations.

nodes.label: Win-Win Thinking
nodes.source_location: Chapter 11: The era of network competition
nodes.rationale: Breaking from adversarial buyer/supplier relationships so all network partners benefit from cooperation; supplier development reduces supplier cost rather than profit.

nodes.label: Open-Book Accounting
nodes.source_location: Chapter 11: The era of network competition
nodes.rationale: Sharing cost data upstream and downstream so each partner's profit is visible to others, a manifestation of transparency in collaborative networks.

nodes.label: Prisoner's Dilemma
nodes.source_location: Chapter 11: The era of network competition
nodes.rationale: Game-theory analogy demonstrating that distrust drives adversarial (sub-optimal) outcomes whereas mutual trust and cooperation yield the best joint result for supply chain partners.

nodes.label: Seven Major Business Transformations
nodes.source_location: Chapter 11: The era of network competition
nodes.rationale: Shifts required for network competition: supplier- to customer-centric, push to pull, inventory to information, transactions to relationships, trucks-and-sheds to end-to-end, functions to processes, stand-alone to network rivalry.

nodes.label: From Push to Pull (Demand-Driven)
nodes.source_location: Chapter 11: The era of network competition
nodes.rationale: Moving from production-push level scheduling to a demand-pull (Kanban) philosophy where nothing is made or moved until there is demand, requiring high resource flexibility.

nodes.label: Customer-Centric / Outside-In Supply Chain
nodes.source_location: Chapter 11: The era of network competition
nodes.rationale: Designing supply chains from the customer backwards and demand-driven, making agility rather than cost the key design driver.

nodes.label: From Functions to Processes
nodes.source_location: Chapter 11: The era of network competition
nodes.rationale: Reorganising around cross-functional, market-facing key business processes that create customer value, rather than around vertical hierarchical functions.

nodes.label: From 3PL to 4PL (Lead Logistics Provider)
nodes.source_location: Chapter 11: The era of network competition
nodes.rationale: Third-party providers deliver logistics activities; the fourth-party provider (Accenture concept) integrates and manages the whole end-to-end chain by assembling a coalition of best-of-breed 3PLs.

nodes.label: T-Shaped Manager
nodes.source_location: Chapter 11: The era of network competition
nodes.rationale: A supply chain manager with deep functional specialism (the vertical bar) plus broad cross-functional understanding (the horizontal bar) needed to manage horizontally across firms.

nodes.label: Supply Chain Talent
nodes.source_location: Chapter 11: The era of network competition
nodes.rationale: Recognition that agile, adaptive supply chains depend on people and skills as much as technology; leading firms actively develop supply chain capabilities and education.

nodes.label: Lean Practices / JIT
nodes.source_location: Chapter 10: Managing risk in the supply chain
nodes.rationale: Just-in-time, inventory-reduction efficiency practices that excel in stable conditions but can increase vulnerability under demand volatility, motivating combination with agile response.

nodes.label: Fourth-Party Logistics (4PL)
nodes.source_location: Chapter 11: The era of network competition
nodes.rationale: A hybrid organisation, often a joint venture, that orchestrates an entire supply chain by integrating systems and managing multiple 3PL asset providers against agreed service and cost goals.

nodes.label: Loosely Coupled Network
nodes.source_location: Chapter 11: The era of network competition
nodes.rationale: A flexible network (e.g. Li & Fung) where an orchestrator specifies what each supplier must achieve but not how, enabling rapid switching of sources and high flexibility despite high coordination costs.

nodes.label: Responsive Organisation
nodes.source_location: Chapter 12: Overcoming the barriers to supply chain integration
nodes.rationale: An organisation that places the customer at the centre and designs all systems and procedures to improve the speed and reliability of response.

nodes.label: Logistics Vision Statement
nodes.source_location: Chapter 12: Overcoming the barriers to supply chain integration
nodes.rationale: A statement giving clear direction on how the firm intends to use logistics and supply chain management to create customer value and build competitive advantage.

nodes.label: Functional (Vertical) Organisation
nodes.source_location: Chapter 12: Overcoming the barriers to supply chain integration
nodes.rationale: A conventional input-focused, budget-driven structure divided by function (purchasing, production, sales) whose vertical silos impede integrated logistics and process management.

nodes.label: Horizontal (Market-Facing) Organisation
nodes.source_location: Chapter 12: Overcoming the barriers to supply chain integration
nodes.rationale: An output-focused, market-driven structure organised around processes, flat and de-layered, built on multi-functional teams and guided by market-based performance metrics.

nodes.label: Order Fulfilment Group
nodes.source_location: Chapter 12: Overcoming the barriers to supply chain integration
nodes.rationale: A cross-functional, co-located team that manages the order-to-cash process together, dramatically reducing order cycle times and resolving service problems faster.

nodes.label: Customer Order Management System
nodes.source_location: Chapter 12: Overcoming the barriers to supply chain integration
nodes.rationale: A market-driven planning framework that links the information system with the physical flow of materials to fulfil demand, integrating forecasts, requirements plans, and current orders.

nodes.label: Pipeline Cost Transparency
nodes.source_location: Chapter 12: Overcoming the barriers to supply chain integration
nodes.rationale: The ability to measure costs of material flows across functional areas; conventional functional costing obscures the true cost-to-serve different customers.

nodes.label: Competitive Benchmarking
nodes.source_location: Chapter 12: Overcoming the barriers to supply chain integration
nodes.rationale: Continuous measurement of products, services, processes and practices against best competitors and recognised leaders, including non-competing industries, to outperform rather than match rivals.

nodes.label: Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)
nodes.source_location: Chapter 12: Overcoming the barriers to supply chain integration
nodes.rationale: A small set of critical, often non-financial, measures that contribute disproportionately to success or failure in the marketplace and derive from strategic goals.

nodes.label: Balanced Scorecard
nodes.source_location: Chapter 12: Overcoming the barriers to supply chain integration
nodes.rationale: A measurement framework of key (mostly non-financial) performance indicators derived from strategy, adapted into a logistics scorecard built around Better, Faster, Cheaper, Closer.

nodes.label: Logistics Scorecard (Better, Faster, Cheaper, Closer)
nodes.source_location: Chapter 12: Overcoming the barriers to supply chain integration
nodes.rationale: A focused set of measures across four outcomes of logistics-derived customer value: service quality (Better), time (Faster), cost (Cheaper), and relationships (Closer).

nodes.label: Sustainable Supply Chain
nodes.source_location: Chapter 13: Creating a sustainable supply chain
nodes.rationale: A supply chain managed across the whole product life cycle to reduce environmental and resource impact while improving profitability through better use of resources.

nodes.label: Triple Bottom Line
nodes.source_location: Chapter 13: Creating a sustainable supply chain
nodes.rationale: The 3Ps of people, profit and planet: examining the impact of business decisions on society, the economy, and the environment simultaneously.

nodes.label: Carbon Footprint
nodes.source_location: Chapter 13: Creating a sustainable supply chain
nodes.rationale: The greenhouse gas emissions (CO2, methane, etc.) associated with an activity; rising due to globalised, transport-intensive supply chains and increasingly subject to carbon pricing.

nodes.label: Greenhouse Gases
nodes.source_location: Chapter 13: Creating a sustainable supply chain
nodes.rationale: Emissions such as carbon dioxide, methane and nitrous oxide whose rising levels from industrial activity are widely seen as a major cause of climate change.

nodes.label: Transport Intensity
nodes.source_location: Chapter 13: Creating a sustainable supply chain
nodes.rationale: The miles/kilometres travelled per unit of product shipped; correlates with the supply chain's carbon footprint and is raised by global sourcing and JIT.

nodes.label: Eco-Efficiency
nodes.source_location: Chapter 13: Creating a sustainable supply chain
nodes.rationale: Achieving both economic and environmental benefit simultaneously, e.g. reducing transport intensity to cut cost and emissions together.

nodes.label: Food Miles
nodes.source_location: Chapter 13: Creating a sustainable supply chain
nodes.rationale: How far food travels from origin to final consumption and the associated carbon impact, a growing consumer and ethical concern.

nodes.label: Peak Oil
nodes.source_location: Chapter 13: Creating a sustainable supply chain
nodes.rationale: Hubbert's idea that oil production follows a bell curve; once the peak passes, supply declines while demand grows, driving fuel prices up and pressuring energy-intensive supply chains.

nodes.label: Reverse Logistics
nodes.source_location: Chapter 13: Creating a sustainable supply chain
nodes.rationale: The process of bringing products back for end-of-life, recall, repair, reuse or recycling; an opportunity to cut both cost and carbon footprint.

nodes.label: Closed-Loop Supply Chain
nodes.source_location: Chapter 13: Creating a sustainable supply chain
nodes.rationale: A supply chain designed to enable high levels of reuse and recycling, with products designed for end-of-life recovery and networks that minimise resource use.

nodes.label: Reduce, Reuse, Recycle (3Rs)
nodes.source_location: Chapter 13: Creating a sustainable supply chain
nodes.rationale: The three Rs of sustainable supply chain management; consuming fewer resources improves both environmental impact and profitability.

nodes.label: Resource Footprint
nodes.source_location: Chapter 13: Creating a sustainable supply chain
nodes.rationale: The wider impact of supply chain decisions on scarce resources across the value chain (design, source, make, deliver, return), beyond just carbon emissions.

nodes.label: Sustainable Sourcing
nodes.source_location: Chapter 13: Creating a sustainable supply chain
nodes.rationale: A best-practice procurement element recognising that 40-80% of a firm's carbon footprint lies upstream, so supplier choice strongly affects resource consumption.

nodes.label: Water Footprint
nodes.source_location: Chapter 13: Creating a sustainable supply chain
nodes.rationale: The volume of water consumed across a supply chain's production processes; measured and reduced as water shortages make it potentially the 'next oil'.

nodes.label: Emission Trading Scheme / Cap and Trade
nodes.source_location: Chapter 13: Creating a sustainable supply chain
nodes.rationale: Regulatory mechanisms (e.g. EU ETS) that cap carbon emissions and let firms trade allowances, forcing companies to price and manage their supply chain emissions.
nodes.norm_label: emission trading scheme / cap and trade

nodes.label: Shared Distribution / Empty Miles
nodes.source_location: Chapter 13: Creating a sustainable supply chain
nodes.rationale: Collaborative transport arrangements (even with competitors) that match available truck space with shipment needs to cut empty running, costs and emissions.

nodes.label: Traffic Congestion and Infrastructure
nodes.source_location: Chapter 13: Creating a sustainable supply chain
nodes.rationale: Gridlock from inadequate roads, ports and railways relative to economic activity, driven by global trade, lack of capacity and JIT, adding cost and emissions.

nodes.label: Smart Logistics / Intelligent Transport
nodes.source_location: Chapter 13: Creating a sustainable supply chain
nodes.rationale: Combining vertical and horizontal collaboration with advanced ICT (dynamic routing, intelligent agents, cross-docking, logistics platforms) to manage constrained capacity under uncertain demand.

nodes.label: Emerging Mega-Trends
nodes.source_location: Chapter 14: The supply chain of the future
nodes.rationale: Large-scale demographic and wealth-redistribution shifts (population growth, urbanisation, rising emerging-economy middle classes) that will reshape future supply chains.

nodes.label: City Logistics / Mega-Cities
nodes.source_location: Chapter 14: The supply chain of the future
nodes.rationale: City-specific supply chain solutions, often using edge-of-city logistics platforms, needed to serve growing mega-cities with inadequate infrastructure.

nodes.label: Shifting Centres of Gravity
nodes.source_location: Chapter 14: The supply chain of the future
nodes.rationale: The pull of demand and supply that determines optimal facility locations; shifting due to demographics, wealth redistribution and volatile input costs, requiring flexible network redesign.

nodes.label: Multi-Channel Distribution
nodes.source_location: Chapter 14: The supply chain of the future
nodes.rationale: Use of multiple routes to market (stores, internet, telephone, home delivery), ideally served by shared logistics infrastructure with consistent customer experience and single inventories.

nodes.label: Last-Mile / Home Delivery
nodes.source_location: Chapter 14: The supply chain of the future
nodes.rationale: The challenge of delivering small (often single-case) online orders to specific addresses cost-effectively, eased by agreed delivery times and dynamic vehicle routing.

nodes.label: Shift to a Buyer's Market
nodes.source_location: Chapter 14: The supply chain of the future
nodes.rationale: The power shift from supplier to customer, accelerated by the Internet which democratises the supply chain by placing the consumer at the centre of the network.

nodes.label: Structural Flexibility
nodes.source_location: Chapter 14: The supply chain of the future
nodes.rationale: The ability of a supply chain to adapt or reconfigure its architecture in response to discontinuous change on the demand or supply side, beyond dynamic volume/mix flexibility.

nodes.label: Dynamic Flexibility
nodes.source_location: Chapter 14: The supply chain of the future
nodes.rationale: The ability to respond rapidly to demand changes in volume or mix for existing products, linked to set-up time reduction and flexible manufacturing systems.

nodes.label: Visibility and Information Sharing
nodes.source_location: Chapter 14: The supply chain of the future
nodes.rationale: Seeing changes upstream and downstream across the pipeline; a key enabler of structural flexibility and collaborative working relationships.

nodes.label: Inter-Operability of Processes and Systems
nodes.source_location: Chapter 14: The supply chain of the future
nodes.rationale: Standard processes and information systems that let supply chain nodes and links be plugged together in various ways to construct specific solutions quickly and cheaply.

nodes.label: Open Innovation / Access to Knowledge and Talent
nodes.source_location: Chapter 14: The supply chain of the future
nodes.rationale: Turning to external sources of knowledge, technology and talent via sharing agreements to provide adaptive product and process innovation capabilities.
nodes.norm_label: open innovation / access to knowledge and talent

nodes.label: Virtual Network Supply Chain
nodes.source_location: Chapter 14: The supply chain of the future
nodes.rationale: Tomorrow's information-based, customer-value-oriented model of virtual networks replacing the inventory-based, low-cost-production model of independent entities.

nodes.label: Rapid Manufacturing / Additive Fabrication
nodes.source_location: Chapter 14: The supply chain of the future
nodes.rationale: Building products layer by layer (digital manufacturing/3D fabrication) enabling cost-effective local-for-local production, late-stage customisation, no finished-goods inventory and minimal waste.

nodes.label: Local-for-Local Manufacturing
nodes.source_location: Chapter 14: The supply chain of the future
nodes.rationale: Bringing supply closer to demand using smaller, flexible facilities (e.g. mini-mills, rapid manufacturing), driven by carbon and rising transport costs.

nodes.label: Small Footprint Supply Chain
nodes.source_location: Chapter 14: The supply chain of the future
nodes.rationale: A supply chain that uses fewer resources yet is more flexible and better able to serve local markets, replacing large-scale centralised structures in a resource-constrained world.

nodes.label: Network Optimisation
nodes.source_location: Chapter 13: Creating a sustainable supply chain
nodes.rationale: Models that determine distribution network shape; a new generation accounts for carbon footprint alongside conventional cost.

nodes.label: Core Competencies and Outsourcing
nodes.source_location: Chapter 12: Overcoming the barriers to supply chain integration
nodes.rationale: Focusing on value-chain activities where the firm has differential advantage and outsourcing the rest to partners, co-makers and logistics service providers.

nodes.label: Logistics as the Vehicle for Change
nodes.source_location: Chapter 12: Overcoming the barriers to supply chain integration
nodes.rationale: The argument that globalisation and complex flows make logistics the driving force for shifting organisations from a functional to a process focus, led from the top.
nodes.norm_label: logistics as the vehicle for change

nodes.label: Grant, Trautrims & Wong: Sustainable Logistics and Supply Chain Management
nodes.author: David B. Grant; Alexander Trautrims; Chee Yew Wong
nodes.rationale: Practitioner text on sustainable logistics: green freight/warehousing, sustainable procurement, reverse logistics/circular economy, CSR.
nodes.norm_label: grant, trautrims & wong: sustainable logistics and supply chain management

nodes.label: Freight Transport
nodes.rationale: Movement of goods through the supply chain; a major contributor to energy use and greenhouse gas emissions, with road transport dominant in tonne-kilometres.

nodes.label: Transport Emissions
nodes.rationale: Greenhouse gases and pollutants produced by freight transport, stemming mainly from fuel combustion and rising with the growth of road transport.

nodes.label: Transport Externalities
nodes.rationale: Wider societal impacts of freight transport beyond atmospheric emissions, including traffic noise, accidents and casualties, and effects on transport workers.

nodes.label: Freight Transport Modes
nodes.rationale: The available means of moving freight (road, rail, sea, air, pipeline), each differing in cost, speed, flexibility and emissions per tonne-kilometre.

nodes.label: Road Freight
nodes.rationale: Most flexible and accessible freight mode, dominant in inland tonne-kilometres but the largest source of freight emissions; classified into FTL/LTL and LCV/HGV.

nodes.label: Rail Freight
nodes.rationale: Slow, fixed-infrastructure inland mode suited to heavy bulk loads; generally greener than road but dependent on journey characteristics and electricity source.

nodes.label: Sea Freight
nodes.rationale: Slow, cheap mode for low-value high-volume goods with low emissions per tonne-km, but burning highly pollutant bunker fuel over long journeys.

nodes.label: Air Freight
nodes.rationale: Fastest long-distance mode but the highest carbon emitter per tonne-km, suited to high-value-density or short-lead-time goods.

nodes.label: Transport Mode Choice
nodes.rationale: Selection of freight mode driven by operational factors, cost and service requirements, product characteristics and consignment factors.

nodes.label: CO2 Equivalent (CO2e)
nodes.rationale: Carbon-dioxide-equivalence measure used in company greenhouse gas reporting to make emissions from different pollutants comparable across transport modes.

nodes.label: Modal Shift
nodes.rationale: Shifting freight toward less-polluting transport modes as a strategy to reduce emissions, subject to infrastructure access and economic viability.

nodes.label: Multi-modal Transport
nodes.rationale: Use of more than one transport mode through a consignment's journey, requiring handling between modes.

nodes.label: Intermodal Transport
nodes.rationale: Design of solutions where infrastructure and equipment (containers, swap-bodies, RORO, terminals) are prepared for seamless transfer across transport modes.

nodes.label: Containerization
nodes.rationale: Standardization of pallets and shipping containers (TEU) that enables fast, low-labour transfer of goods between rail, road and sea modes.

nodes.label: Local Sourcing
nodes.rationale: Sourcing goods closer to the point of consumption to minimize freight transport emissions, reduce supply chain risk and improve responsiveness.

nodes.label: Nearshoring
nodes.rationale: Locating manufacturing in low-wage countries near the place of consumption to cut lead times and supply chain uncertainty while limiting transport.

nodes.label: Alternative Fuels
nodes.rationale: Non-conventional transport fuels (biofuel, LNG, hydrogen, electricity) whose environmental balance depends heavily on production source and infrastructure.

nodes.label: Biofuel
nodes.rationale: Crop- or waste-derived fuel promoted to cut emissions but raising food-versus-fuel ethics, land-use and biodiversity concerns; used as aviation drop-in fuel.

nodes.label: Hydrogen Fuel
nodes.rationale: Alternative fuel usable in tanks or fuel cells emitting only water; its sustainability depends on the energy source used in its electrolytic production.

nodes.label: Electric Vehicles
nodes.rationale: Vehicles powered by grid electricity, environmentally friendly for short urban travel but with sustainability determined by how the electricity is generated.

nodes.label: Slow Steaming
nodes.rationale: Running ships at lower-than-design speeds to save bunker fuel and emissions, adding journey time but improving schedule reliability.

nodes.label: Vehicle Utilization and Backhauling
nodes.rationale: Loading factor and reduction of empty running through backloading; key driver of both cost and the environmental performance of transport fleets.

nodes.label: Logistics Collaboration
nodes.rationale: Sharing of freight transport between companies, even competitors, to improve vehicle utilization and cut fuel, cost and CO2 (e.g. Nestle and United Biscuits).

nodes.label: Transport Telematics
nodes.rationale: IT systems connecting vehicles to dispatch for dynamic routing, fuel-efficiency monitoring and driver-behaviour tracking, reducing emissions.

nodes.label: Vehicle Design Efficiency
nodes.rationale: Aerodynamic profiling, weight reduction and larger vehicles (gigaliners) within regulated dimensions to lower fuel consumption and emissions.

nodes.label: Autonomous Vehicles
nodes.rationale: Driverless vehicles expected to cut accidents and improve fuel efficiency, but risking urban sprawl, more journeys and loss of driver employment.

nodes.label: Traffic Congestion
nodes.rationale: Insufficient infrastructure capacity causing vehicles to queue and burn excess fuel; addressed by reducing demand, congestion charging or off-peak delivery.

nodes.label: Urban Consolidation Centre
nodes.rationale: Facility used to bundle deliveries for city-centre and congested sites, reducing the number of delivery journeys in urban areas.

nodes.label: Lifecycle Assessment
nodes.rationale: Holistic accounting of inputs and outputs from raw material extraction, manufacturing, construction, use and disposal to assess true environmental impact.

nodes.label: Sustainable Warehousing
nodes.rationale: Reducing the environmental and social impact of warehouses across construction and operations while supporting supply chain strategy.

nodes.label: Warehouse Roles and Functions
nodes.rationale: Roles warehouses play as supply chain nodes: storage, cross-docking, fulfilment, value-added activities, consolidation and JIT buffering.

nodes.label: Fulfilment Centre
nodes.rationale: Warehouse where orders are picked, packed and dispatched to customers, often with high return rates requiring specialized manual processing.

nodes.label: Inventory Centralization
nodes.rationale: Consolidating stock into fewer sites to cut safety stock via the square root law, trading inventory cost against increased transport kilometres.

nodes.label: Square Root Law of Inventory
nodes.rationale: Rule that centralized safety stock equals decentralized stock divided by the square root of the number of locations (e.g. 10 to 1 site cuts safety stock 68%).

nodes.label: Warehouse Location
nodes.rationale: Choice of warehouse number and sites near the centre of gravity, balancing transport and inventory costs against land, labour and modal access.

nodes.label: Materials Handling Equipment
nodes.rationale: Forklifts, conveyors, cranes and automated systems consuming operational energy; selection trades throughput, flexibility and storage density.

nodes.label: Warehouse Automation (AS/RS)
nodes.rationale: Automated Storage/Retrieval Systems giving higher throughput and storage density but lower flexibility than human labour.

nodes.label: Green Building Certification (LEED/BREEAM)
nodes.rationale: LEED and BREEAM frameworks scoring warehouse sustainability across weighted categories to award certification classes.

nodes.label: Embodied Energy and Carbon
nodes.rationale: Energy and carbon embedded in construction materials and processes, traded off against operational emissions over a building's lifespan.

nodes.label: Warehouse Energy Efficiency
nodes.rationale: Reducing operational energy via insulation, temperature zoning, efficient LED lighting, rooflights and air circulation to cut emissions and cost.

nodes.label: On-site Green Energy Generation
nodes.rationale: Harnessing solar, wind, biomass, kinetic and thermal energy at warehouse sites, often supported by feed-in tariffs and renewable obligations.

nodes.label: Warehouse Social Sustainability
nodes.rationale: Social dimension of warehousing covering health, safety, ergonomic and ageing-workforce design and labour conditions (e.g. Sports Direct case).

nodes.label: Data Centre Sustainability
nodes.rationale: Energy efficiency of electronic data storage facilities, benchmarked by Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE), with savings in cooling, servers and power supply.

nodes.label: Logistics Outsourcing
nodes.rationale: Use of third-party logistics providers for warehousing and contract logistics, affecting long-term incentives to invest in sustainability measures.

nodes.label: Cleaner Production
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5: Product design, cleaner production and packaging
nodes.rationale: Managing product design, production and packaging to prevent pollution at source rather than controlling it after the fact (Hart's natural resource-based view).

nodes.label: Sustainable Product Design
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5: Product design, cleaner production and packaging
nodes.rationale: Designing products and packaging to reduce resource use, hazardous substances, pollution and waste while meeting cost and customer needs.

nodes.label: Pollution Prevention
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5: Product design, cleaner production and packaging
nodes.rationale: First step of the natural resource-based view: shifting away from end-of-pipe pollution control toward preventing pollution at its source.

nodes.label: Natural Resource-Based View
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5: Product design, cleaner production and packaging
nodes.rationale: Hart's strategic framework linking competitive advantage to a firm's relationship with the natural environment, guiding sustainable transformation.

nodes.label: Green Logistics
nodes.rationale: Reducing the environmental impact of logistics activities including freight transport and warehousing through cleaner modes, fuels and operations.

nodes.label: Product Stewardship
nodes.source_location: Ch5 Product Design, Cleaner Production and Packaging
nodes.rationale: Stage of environmental strategy that minimizes environmental impacts across a product's full lifecycle, from raw material extraction to end-of-life management.

nodes.label: Clean Technology
nodes.source_location: Ch5 Product Design, Cleaner Production and Packaging
nodes.rationale: Investment in technologies such as biotechnology, cleaner chemical engineering and renewable energy that make the entire supply chain sustainable.

nodes.label: Design for Environment (Eco-design)
nodes.source_location: Ch5 Product Design, Cleaner Production and Packaging
nodes.rationale: Integrating environmental considerations at the product design stage, where over 80 per cent of a product's environmental impact is determined, reconciling environmental, economic and social goals.

nodes.label: Circular Economy
nodes.source_location: Ch5 Product Design, Cleaner Production and Packaging
nodes.rationale: Ellen MacArthur Foundation concept emphasizing restoration (technical) and regeneration (biological) by design to keep products, components and materials at highest utilization and value.

nodes.label: Selection of Materials
nodes.source_location: Ch5 Product Design, Cleaner Production and Packaging
nodes.rationale: Choosing recycled, non-hazardous and environmentally friendlier raw materials for product design, balancing supply availability, reliability, quality, lead time and cost.

nodes.label: Hazardous Substances
nodes.source_location: Ch5 Product Design, Cleaner Production and Packaging
nodes.rationale: Materials such as arsenic, mercury, lead, cadmium, chromium, PCBs, POPs and PBB/PBDE that are toxic or carcinogenic and are being phased out via banned/restricted material lists.

nodes.label: Conflict Minerals
nodes.source_location: Ch5 Product Design, Cleaner Production and Packaging
nodes.rationale: Minerals such as tantalite (coltan), cassiterite (tin), gold and wolframite (tungsten) whose mining finances conflicts; in diamonds called conflict or blood diamonds.

nodes.label: Restricted/Banned Materials List
nodes.source_location: Ch5 Product Design, Cleaner Production and Packaging
nodes.rationale: A list of chemical substances a company eliminates or keeps below required concentrations, integrated into supplier codes of conduct and environmental management procedures.

nodes.label: Environmental Management System (EMS)
nodes.source_location: Ch5 Product Design, Cleaner Production and Packaging
nodes.rationale: Formal system and database integrating procedures for training, monitoring, reporting and improving environmental performance to internal and external stakeholders.

nodes.label: ISO 14001
nodes.rationale: The most widely used international standard for environmental management systems, emphasizing commitment to compliance and continuous improvement; certified by independent auditors.

nodes.label: Eco-Management and Audit Scheme (EMAS)
nodes.source_location: Ch5 Product Design, Cleaner Production and Packaging
nodes.rationale: Europe's second most popular EMS standard, requiring legal compliance and obligatory public audits every three years to guarantee certification.

nodes.label: Waste Minimization
nodes.source_location: Ch5 Product Design, Cleaner Production and Packaging
nodes.rationale: EPA waste-prevention approach focusing on on-site reduction of waste at source via changes in raw materials, technology, practices and product design, plus off-site recycling.

nodes.label: Zero Waste
nodes.source_location: Ch5 Product Design, Cleaner Production and Packaging
nodes.rationale: A philosophy and target for waste minimization where no waste is sent to landfill, redesigning product/resource lifecycles to produce no waste or transform all waste into something useful.

nodes.label: Green Productivity
nodes.source_location: Ch5 Product Design, Cleaner Production and Packaging
nodes.rationale: Asian Productivity Organization strategy for enhancing productivity and environmental performance together to improve socio-economic development; akin to resource efficiency.

nodes.label: Industrial Symbiosis
nodes.source_location: Ch5 Product Design, Cleaner Production and Packaging
nodes.rationale: Eco-industrial development applying industrial ecology so by-product resources (waste, heat, water) of one industry are used by others, exemplified by Kalundborg Symbiosis.

nodes.label: End-of-Pipe Solution
nodes.source_location: Ch5 Product Design, Cleaner Production and Packaging
nodes.rationale: Pollution-control approach that treats waste after it is generated and merely shifts pollutants from one environmental medium to another, contrasted with preventive cleaner production.

nodes.label: Lean Production and 5S
nodes.source_location: Ch5 Product Design, Cleaner Production and Packaging
nodes.rationale: Lean tools such as 5S good housekeeping, total preventive maintenance and statistical process control that create opportunities for implementing cleaner production and waste reduction.

nodes.label: Factor 4 and Factor 10
nodes.source_location: Ch5 Product Design, Cleaner Production and Packaging
nodes.rationale: Ambitious resource-productivity targets: Factor 4 means twice as productive with half the resources; Factor 10 means ten times more productive with the same input.

nodes.label: Zero Emission
nodes.source_location: Ch5 Product Design, Cleaner Production and Packaging
nodes.rationale: A driving benchmark goal of emitting no pollutants; not practically attainable but used to spur serious environmental improvement efforts across industries.

nodes.label: Sustainable Packaging
nodes.source_location: Ch5 Product Design, Cleaner Production and Packaging
nodes.rationale: Designing packaging for minimum volume/weight, reuse, recovery and recycling while minimizing environmental impact and hazardous substances, per EU Directive 94/62/EC.

nodes.label: Biodegradable/Compostable Packaging
nodes.source_location: Ch5 Product Design, Cleaner Production and Packaging
nodes.rationale: Packaging capable of physical, chemical, thermal or biological decomposition into CO2, biomass and water; e.g. home-compostable packaging from maize, sugar cane or starch.

nodes.label: Sustainable Consumption
nodes.source_location: Ch5 Product Design, Cleaner Production and Packaging
nodes.rationale: Use of services and products meeting basic needs and improving quality of life while minimizing natural resources, toxics and emissions over the lifecycle, not jeopardizing future generations.

nodes.label: Energy Efficiency Standards and Labels
nodes.source_location: Ch5 Product Design, Cleaner Production and Packaging
nodes.rationale: Mandatory and voluntary standards and labels (e.g. Energy Star) requiring household products to meet energy-efficiency levels and guiding consumers toward greener products.

nodes.label: Product Service System
nodes.source_location: Ch5 Product Design, Cleaner Production and Packaging
nodes.rationale: Business models such as leasing, buy-back and pay-by-wash that encourage prolonged use, repair and reuse of products, creating closed-loop reverse logistics processes.

nodes.label: Product Durability and Longevity
nodes.source_location: Ch5 Product Design, Cleaner Production and Packaging
nodes.rationale: Designing durable products and replaceable components to prolong life and avoid wastage, opposing fast-fashion and frequent-replacement models (e.g. Patagonia's 'buy less, buy used').

nodes.label: Cradle-to-Cradle Design
nodes.source_location: Ch5 Product Design, Cleaner Production and Packaging
nodes.rationale: Design concept that eliminates waste altogether by treating waste as food and energy and mirroring nature's biological metabolism in a technical metabolism of industrial materials.

nodes.label: Porter Hypothesis
nodes.source_location: Ch5 Product Design, Cleaner Production and Packaging
nodes.rationale: Hypothesis that stricter environmental regulation can create a win-win by driving innovation and competitiveness that outweighs the short-run costs of compliance.

nodes.label: RoHS Directive
nodes.source_location: Ch5 Product Design, Cleaner Production and Packaging
nodes.rationale: EU directive restricting six hazardous materials (lead, mercury, cadmium, hexavalent chromium, PBB, PBDE) in electrical and electronic equipment.

nodes.label: WEEE Directive
nodes.source_location: Ch5 Product Design, Cleaner Production and Packaging
nodes.rationale: EU Waste Electrical and Electronic Equipment Directive setting collection, recycling and recovery targets for electrical goods to tackle toxic e-waste, working alongside RoHS.

nodes.label: REACH Regulation
nodes.source_location: Ch5 Product Design, Cleaner Production and Packaging
nodes.rationale: EU regulation on Registration, Evaluation, Authorisation and Restriction of Chemicals requiring industry to register chemical properties with ECHA and substitute the most dangerous chemicals.

nodes.label: Eco-design Directive
nodes.source_location: Ch5 Product Design, Cleaner Production and Packaging
nodes.rationale: EU framework directive 2009/125/EC setting minimum energy-in-use requirements for energy-using and energy-related products, enabling CE marking and energy savings.

nodes.label: Producer Responsibility (EPR)
nodes.source_location: Ch5 Product Design, Cleaner Production and Packaging
nodes.rationale: Obligation (e.g. UK Packaging Waste Regulations 2007) making producers pay a share of recovery and recycling costs of their packaging, allocated across the packaging supply chain.

nodes.label: Product/Organizational Environmental Footprint (PEF/OEF)
nodes.source_location: Ch5 Product Design, Cleaner Production and Packaging
nodes.rationale: European Commission harmonized, multi-criterion, supply-chain-wide lifecycle assessment methods to measure and communicate the environmental performance of products and organizations.

nodes.label: Eco-labels and Certifications
nodes.rationale: Independent labels (e.g. EU Flower, Oeko-Tex, Fairtrade, MSC, FSC, LEED, Carbon Trust) that signal a supplier meets defined sustainability standards and shift auditing to external bodies.

nodes.label: Sustainable Procurement
nodes.rationale: Inclusion of environmental and social sustainability issues in procurement strategy, sourcing decisions and supplier selection across the triple bottom line.

nodes.label: Green Procurement
nodes.rationale: Environmentally sustainable procurement focused on reducing the environmental footprint of sourced goods and supplier operations.

nodes.label: Socially Responsible Procurement
nodes.rationale: Procurement addressing social impacts such as child labour, modern slavery, human rights and fair labour, driven by consumer reaction and legislation (e.g. UK Modern Slavery Act).

nodes.label: Public Procurement
nodes.rationale: Government buying (around a seventh of EU GDP) that can lead green and sustainable procurement and create base demand for more sustainable products and services.

nodes.label: Supplier Tiers
nodes.rationale: Structuring of suppliers into first-tier (closest to the focal firm), second-tier and beyond when mapping supply chains, complicating monitoring of sub-suppliers.

nodes.label: Supply Chain Visibility and Transparency
nodes.rationale: Visibility needed to monitor sustainability, manage risk and disruption, conduct supply chain audits and improve operations such as inventory levels.

nodes.label: Kraljic Matrix
nodes.rationale: Kraljic's (1983) framework categorizing supplied items by profit impact and supply risk into strategic, bottleneck, leverage and non-critical, guiding sustainability integration per category.

nodes.label: Internal Adaptation Levels (Resistant to Constructive)
nodes.rationale: Walton et al's (1998) four categories of organizational adaptation of sustainable practices: resistant, reactive, receptive and constructive.

nodes.label: Greenwashing
nodes.rationale: Paying lip service to sustainability for advertisement purposes without genuine commitment, a barrier that undermines legitimacy of sustainability efforts.

nodes.label: Cost-Benefit and Cost-Effectiveness Analysis
nodes.rationale: Methods using lifecycle costs to compare purchasing options: cost-benefit monetarizes external effects on welfare, while cost-effectiveness compares solutions against a target unit of impact.

nodes.label: Multi-Criteria Supplier Analysis
nodes.rationale: Supplier comparison where sustainability performance is a knock-out criterion, monetary value, or a weighted score within an overall scored decision.

nodes.label: Labels and Certifications
nodes.rationale: Schemes (Fairtrade, FSC, MSC, LEED, ISO 14001) that set standards and shift auditing/monitoring burdens off the buyer, simplifying sustainability in procurement.

nodes.label: Internalization of External Costs
nodes.rationale: Making firms bear the external (environmental/social) costs of their activities so sustainability is reflected in purchasing decisions.

nodes.label: Recycling
nodes.rationale: Collecting used products, components or materials and reprocessing them into raw materials or new products, reducing use of virgin resources.

nodes.label: Linear Economy
nodes.rationale: Traditional 'take, make and dispose' model that consumes virgin materials and generates waste and pollution; the antithesis of the circular economy.

nodes.label: Waste and Resource Management
nodes.rationale: Reframing of waste management toward conserving resources, prioritizing resource efficiency and aiming for zero waste.

nodes.label: Waste Hierarchy
nodes.rationale: Hierarchical ranking of waste options by environmental friendliness: reduce, reuse, recycle, recovery (energy), then disposal/landfill as last resort.

nodes.label: Reduce (Source Reduction)
nodes.rationale: Most environmentally friendly option; using fewer natural resources, hazardous materials and energy through design, cleaner production and consumer behaviour.

nodes.label: Reuse
nodes.rationale: Most resource-efficient direct product recovery option; using a product or its parts again via sharing, refurbishment, repair or remanufacture.

nodes.label: Energy Recovery (Waste-to-Energy)
nodes.rationale: Creating energy as electricity or heat from incineration of waste when materials cannot be reused or recycled; also called energy-from-waste.

nodes.label: Landfill / Disposal
nodes.rationale: Last and least desirable waste option; non-degradable and toxic materials pollute ground waters and create methane, a greenhouse gas.

nodes.label: Biological Nutrient Cycle
nodes.rationale: Circular-economy cycle of renewable materials regenerating biochemical feedstock and energy, e.g. fertilizers, feeds and biogas via anaerobic digestion.

nodes.label: Technical Nutrient Cycle
nodes.rationale: Circular-economy cycle that recovers and restores products, parts and materials without consumption, keeping them at highest utility and value.

nodes.label: Product Recovery Management (PRM)
nodes.rationale: Management of all used and discarded products, components and materials under a manufacturer's responsibility to recover as much economic and ecological value as reasonably possible (Thierry et al, 1995).

nodes.label: Repair
nodes.rationale: Product recovery option that returns used products to working order at product level, often without disassembly; quality lower than new.

nodes.label: Refurbishing
nodes.rationale: Product recovery option bringing used products to a specified quality via module-level disassembly, inspection and replacement; quality lower than new.

nodes.label: Remanufacturing
nodes.rationale: Product recovery option bringing used products up to as-new quality through full disassembly to part level, inspection, replacement and reassembly.

nodes.label: Cannibalization
nodes.rationale: Product recovery option recovering a limited set of reusable parts via selective disassembly; remaining parts are recycled or disposed of.

nodes.label: Upcycling
nodes.rationale: Creative reuse that transforms low-value unwanted items into new, higher-value, often handmade and one-of-a-kind products.

nodes.label: Take-Back Scheme
nodes.rationale: Collection scheme where retailers or OEMs accept used products back for repair, remanufacture, refurbishment or recycling, free or for a fee.

nodes.label: Collection Schemes
nodes.rationale: Designs for returning used products (deposit fee, take-back, trade-in, pick-up, public recycling centres) whose design and incentives determine return-flow magnitude.

nodes.label: Deposit Fee Scheme
nodes.rationale: Advanced deposit charged at purchase and refunded when used products or packaging are returned in good condition, common for bottled beverages.

nodes.label: Value Recovery (VR)
nodes.rationale: Concept (Srivastava, 2008) defining collection, inspection/sorting, preprocessing/logistics and distribution network design as functional aspects for recovering economic value.

nodes.label: Design for Disassembly
nodes.rationale: Product design enabling rapid, easy disassembly (e.g. two-way fasteners, parts marking) to support reuse, remanufacture and recycling, as practised by BMW and Xerox.

nodes.label: Hazardous Materials in Recovery
nodes.rationale: Major reverse-logistics problem from toxic substances such as CRT lead glass, mercury in LCDs, heavy-metal circuit boards, lithium batteries and VOCs from photocopiers.

nodes.label: Metal Recycling
nodes.rationale: Pyramid industry processing ferrous and nonferrous scrap (collection, shredding, media separation, melting) into secondary raw material, saving 60-87% of smelting energy.

nodes.label: Plastics Recycling
nodes.rationale: Recycling of PET, HDPE, PVC, LDPE, PP and PS plastics via mechanical recycling (melting/shredding) or feedstock recycling (breaking polymers into monomers).

nodes.label: Kerbside Collection Schemes
nodes.rationale: Household recyclable collection by local authorities using single-stream (co-mingled), twin-stream or kerbside-sort systems, with cost and contamination trade-offs.

nodes.label: Material Recovery Facility (MRF)
nodes.rationale: Facility that sorts and bales collected waste; local authorities pay gate and sorting fees, with contracts and revenue-sharing shaping recycling quality and economics.

nodes.label: Supply Chain Disruption
nodes.source_location: Risk, Resilience and Corporate Social Responsibility
nodes.rationale: Unforeseen event interfering with the normal flow of goods/materials, causing lost productivity, revenue, customers and sometimes lives (Ellis et al, 2010).

nodes.label: Reputational Risk
nodes.source_location: Risk, Resilience and Corporate Social Responsibility
nodes.rationale: Risk of brand damage and lost market share from public distrust, boycotts or breaches of trust, often exposed by NGOs over ethical or environmental misconduct.
