nodes.label: Likelihood-Impact Risk Assessment
nodes.source_location: Risk, Resilience and Corporate Social Responsibility
nodes.rationale: Plotting each risk's probability of occurrence against severity of impact on a graph to prioritize mitigation (low/medium/high priority quartiles).

nodes.label: Risk Mitigation Strategies
nodes.source_location: Risk, Resilience and Corporate Social Responsibility
nodes.rationale: Five common responses: accept, insure, reduce losses, prepare recovery plans, and reduce risk exposure (Knemeyer et al, 2009), matched to likelihood and impact.

nodes.label: Atomic vs Holistic Risk
nodes.source_location: Risk, Resilience and Corporate Social Responsibility
nodes.rationale: Distinction between unsystematic risk affecting a limited part of the chain and systemic risk causing widespread, multi-member disruption.

nodes.label: Normal Accident Theory
nodes.source_location: Risk, Resilience and Corporate Social Responsibility
nodes.rationale: View that failures in complex, tightly coupled systems are 'normal' and unavoidable; coincident failures can cascade unexpectedly into catastrophe.

nodes.label: Cyber Breach and Attack Risk
nodes.source_location: Risk, Resilience and Corporate Social Responsibility
nodes.rationale: Emerging risk from reliance on internet/mobile IT systems, where attacks cause financial, data, IP and reputational losses requiring adaptive security platforms.

nodes.label: Multi-Tier Sustainable SCM
nodes.source_location: Risk, Resilience and Corporate Social Responsibility
nodes.rationale: Strategies (don't bother, indirect, working with third parties, direct) for managing sustainability and reputational risk in lower-tier/sub-suppliers (Tachizawa and Wong, 2014).

nodes.label: Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR)
nodes.source_location: Risk, Resilience and Corporate Social Responsibility
nodes.rationale: Voluntary alignment of business actions with society's objectives and values; encompasses economic, legal, ethical and discretionary expectations (Carroll, 1979).

nodes.label: Business Ethics
nodes.source_location: Risk, Resilience and Corporate Social Responsibility
nodes.rationale: Consideration of the ethical consequences of business actions on society, central to the earlier conception of CSR (Bowen, 1953).

nodes.label: Voluntary Self-Regulation
nodes.source_location: Risk, Resilience and Corporate Social Responsibility
nodes.rationale: Principle that genuine social responsibility must be voluntary rather than legally compelled; self-regulation is seen as more progressive (King and Lenox, 2000).

nodes.label: Stakeholder Balancing
nodes.source_location: Risk, Resilience and Corporate Social Responsibility
nodes.rationale: View of the responsible firm as one whose managers balance a multiplicity of interests (shareholders, employees, suppliers, communities, nation) (Johnson, 1971).

nodes.label: Pyramid of CSR
nodes.source_location: Ch 8: Risk, Resilience and Corporate Social Responsibility
nodes.rationale: Carroll's hierarchy of corporate responsibilities: economic, legal, ethical and philanthropic, each building on the one below.

nodes.label: Economic Responsibility
nodes.source_location: Ch 8: Risk, Resilience and Corporate Social Responsibility
nodes.rationale: The foundational corporate duty to be profitable so the firm can sustain employment and pay tax.

nodes.label: Legal Responsibility
nodes.source_location: Ch 8: Risk, Resilience and Corporate Social Responsibility
nodes.rationale: The duty to obey the law as society's codification of right and wrong.

nodes.label: Ethical Responsibility
nodes.source_location: Ch 8: Risk, Resilience and Corporate Social Responsibility
nodes.rationale: The obligation to do what is right and fair and avoid harm, going beyond mere legal compliance.

nodes.label: Philanthropic Responsibility
nodes.source_location: Ch 8: Risk, Resilience and Corporate Social Responsibility
nodes.rationale: The discretionary duty to be a good corporate citizen through charitable and community contributions.

nodes.label: Fairtrade
nodes.source_location: Ch 8: Risk, Resilience and Corporate Social Responsibility
nodes.rationale: A certification ensuring producers receive at least a minimum price covering sustainable production costs, acting as a safety net when market prices fall.

nodes.label: Child Labour
nodes.source_location: Ch 8: Risk, Resilience and Corporate Social Responsibility
nodes.rationale: A major ethical problem in supply chains such as cocoa, addressed by voluntary commitments like the Harkin-Engel Protocol.

nodes.label: Consequential Ethical Theories
nodes.source_location: Ch 8: Risk, Resilience and Corporate Social Responsibility
nodes.rationale: Theories (egoism, utilitarianism) that judge an act's morality by the ratio of good to evil it produces in its consequences.

nodes.label: Non-Consequential Ethical Theories
nodes.source_location: Ch 8: Risk, Resilience and Corporate Social Responsibility
nodes.rationale: Theories (golden rule, Kant's categorical imperative, Rawls' justice, ethical relativism) that judge morality by duties and rules rather than outcomes.

nodes.label: Moral, Amoral and Immoral Managers
nodes.source_location: Ch 8: Risk, Resilience and Corporate Social Responsibility
nodes.rationale: A typology of managers: immoral (actively opposing ethics), amoral (insensitive to ethics), and moral (adhering to high ethical norms).

nodes.label: Codes of Conduct
nodes.source_location: Ch 8: Risk, Resilience and Corporate Social Responsibility
nodes.rationale: Business principles a corporation expects employees and suppliers to follow, covering labour, governance, supplier practices and ethics.

nodes.label: Corporate Governance
nodes.source_location: Ch 8: Risk, Resilience and Corporate Social Responsibility
nodes.rationale: Principles covering honesty, integrity, legal compliance, anti-bribery, conflict of interest and management of shareholder assets.

nodes.label: UN Global Compact
nodes.source_location: Ch 8: Risk, Resilience and Corporate Social Responsibility
nodes.rationale: A UN initiative offering ten universal principles in human rights, labour, environment and anti-corruption for businesses to adopt.

nodes.label: Sustainability Reporting
nodes.source_location: Ch 8: Risk, Resilience and Corporate Social Responsibility
nodes.rationale: Disclosure of a company's social and environmental impacts to stakeholders following standardized frameworks and quality guidelines.

nodes.label: Global Reporting Initiative (GRI)
nodes.source_location: Ch 8: Risk, Resilience and Corporate Social Responsibility
nodes.rationale: A non-profit providing a comprehensive, standardized sustainability reporting framework to harmonize and clarify non-financial disclosure.

nodes.label: Supplier Code of Conduct
nodes.source_location: Ch 8: Risk, Resilience and Corporate Social Responsibility
nodes.rationale: Standards imposed on suppliers (e.g. HP's based on the EICC) covering labour, health and safety, environment, ethics and management systems.

nodes.label: Electronic Industry Code of Conduct (EICC)
nodes.source_location: Ch 8: Risk, Resilience and Corporate Social Responsibility
nodes.rationale: An electronics-industry code developed by Dell, HP, IBM and contract manufacturers to ensure safe, respectful and environmentally responsible supply chains.

nodes.label: Supplier Audit
nodes.source_location: Ch 8: Risk, Resilience and Corporate Social Responsibility
nodes.rationale: On-site assessment (e.g. Apple's) of records, facilities and policies to verify supplier compliance and detect core violations.

nodes.label: Resource (Sustainability) Risk
nodes.source_location: Ch 8: Risk, Resilience and Corporate Social Responsibility
nodes.rationale: The emerging risk arising from natural resource depletion and competition for scarce resources that can make a supply chain unsustainable.

nodes.label: Corporate Strategy
nodes.source_location: Ch 9: Sustainable Logistics and SCM Strategy
nodes.rationale: The long-term direction and scope of an organization that achieves advantage through resource configuration to meet market and stakeholder needs.

nodes.label: Strategic, Tactical and Operational Planning
nodes.source_location: Ch 9: Sustainable Logistics and SCM Strategy
nodes.rationale: The three hierarchical levels of corporate plans: long-term strategic (5+ years), medium-term tactical (1-5 years) and detailed annual operating plans.

nodes.label: SWOT Analysis
nodes.source_location: Ch 9: Sustainable Logistics and SCM Strategy
nodes.rationale: A situational analysis of a firm's internal Strengths and Weaknesses and external Opportunities and Threats.

nodes.label: PESTLE Analysis
nodes.source_location: Ch 9: Sustainable Logistics and SCM Strategy
nodes.rationale: External environment analysis across Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal and Ecological factors.

nodes.label: Container Fill Optimization
nodes.source_location: Ch 9: Sustainable Logistics and SCM Strategy
nodes.rationale: Packing more product into containers to reduce the number of container movements and related CO2 emissions, constrained by 'cubing out' vs 'weighing out'.

nodes.label: Fuel Switching (Low-Sulphur Fuel)
nodes.source_location: Ch 9: Sustainable Logistics and SCM Strategy
nodes.rationale: Switching ships to lower-sulphur diesel to cut SO2 emissions in port, exemplified by the voluntary Fair Winds Charter in Hong Kong.

nodes.label: Transaction Cost Economics (TCE)
nodes.source_location: Ch 9: Sustainable Logistics and SCM Strategy
nodes.rationale: A theory describing the firm as a governance structure responding to transaction costs from bounded rationality, opportunism, asset specificity and information asymmetry.

nodes.label: Bounded Rationality
nodes.source_location: Ch 9: Sustainable Logistics and SCM Strategy
nodes.rationale: The condition that managers cannot accurately evaluate all decision alternatives due to physical and cognitive constraints.

nodes.label: Opportunism
nodes.source_location: Ch 9: Sustainable Logistics and SCM Strategy
nodes.rationale: The risk that managers exploit a situation to their own advantage, a key behavioural assumption in transaction cost economics.

nodes.label: Asset Specificity
nodes.source_location: Ch 9: Sustainable Logistics and SCM Strategy
nodes.rationale: Investment in resources specific to an exchange that have little value elsewhere, exposing the investor to opportunistic appropriation of economic rent.

nodes.label: Resource-Based View (RBV)
nodes.source_location: Ch 9: Sustainable Logistics and SCM Strategy
nodes.rationale: A theory that a firm's resources and its capability to convert them into value are the keys to sustainable competitive advantage.

nodes.label: Key Competencies for Sustainability
nodes.source_location: Ch 9: Sustainable Logistics and SCM Strategy
nodes.rationale: Wiek et al's five competencies (systems-thinking, anticipatory, normative, strategic, interpersonal) needed to guide sustainability programmes.

nodes.label: Sustainable Supply Chain Management (SSCM)
nodes.source_location: Ch 9: Sustainable Logistics and SCM Strategy
nodes.rationale: The strategic, transparent integration of social, environmental and economic goals across inter-organizational processes, resting on the TBL plus risk, transparency, culture and strategy.

nodes.label: Sustainability Performance Measurement
nodes.source_location: Ch 9: Sustainable Logistics and SCM Strategy
nodes.rationale: Extending traditional cost/time/accuracy logistics metrics to include environmental and CSR measures, since 'you cannot manage what you cannot measure'.

nodes.label: Greenhouse Gas (GHG) Emissions
nodes.source_location: Ch 9: Sustainable Logistics and SCM Strategy
nodes.rationale: A central focus of sustainable supply chain performance measures, driven by climate-change agreements such as the 2015 Paris COP 21 deal.

nodes.label: Environmental Performance Indicators
nodes.source_location: Ch 9: Sustainable Logistics and SCM Strategy
nodes.rationale: ISO 14031 indicators classified as Management (MPI), Operational (OPI) and Environmental Condition (ECI) indicators for measuring environmental performance.

nodes.label: Anaerobic Digestion
nodes.source_location: Ch 9: Sustainable Logistics and SCM Strategy
nodes.rationale: A resource-recovery technology that decomposes organic waste to generate biogas and digestate, diverting waste from landfill and producing renewable energy.

nodes.label: Ten Tenets of Environmental Management
nodes.source_location: Ch 9: Sustainable Logistics and SCM Strategy
nodes.rationale: Barnard and Elliott's criteria (socially desirable, ecologically sustainable, economically viable, technologically feasible, etc.) for assessing whether environmental management is sustainable.

nodes.label: DAPSI(W)R(M) Framework
nodes.source_location: Ch 9: Sustainable Logistics and SCM Strategy
nodes.rationale: A framework linking Drivers, Activities, Pressures, State changes, Impacts on Welfare and Responses as Measures to define and address environmental problems.

nodes.label: Bow-Tie Risk Analysis
nodes.source_location: Ch 9: Sustainable Logistics and SCM Strategy
nodes.rationale: A risk method combining fault tree analysis of causes and event tree analysis of consequences around a central critical event for environmental management.

nodes.label: Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)
nodes.source_location: Ch 9: Sustainable Logistics and SCM Strategy
nodes.rationale: A graphical technique relating an undesired top event to its basic causes using failure probabilities as inputs.

nodes.label: Event Tree Analysis (ETA)
nodes.source_location: Ch 9: Sustainable Logistics and SCM Strategy
nodes.rationale: A graphical model constructing a binary tree of probable consequences and outcome events from an initiating event.

nodes.label: Stakeholder Theory
nodes.source_location: Ch 8: Risk, Resilience and Corporate Social Responsibility
nodes.rationale: The view that a firm's responsibility extends to all stakeholders (employees, customers, community, environment) rather than only shareholders.
